title: AI, Attention, and the Fight for Focus
description: AI can either steal our focus or free it for work that really matters.
contentMarkdown:
# AI, Attention, and the Fight for Focus

I finally picked up *Deep Work* by Cal Newport, even though it's been almost a decade since it first hit shelves back in 2016. What struck me right away was how timely it feels today, right in the thick of the AI revolution that's reshaping everything from how we work to how we spend our free time. The book's ideas about focus and attention suddenly make a lot more sense when you look at them through the lens of tools like Claude, ChatGPT, and the endless stream of AI agents promising to supercharge productivity.

At its heart, *Deep Work* boils down to one core idea: the most valuable work—whether it's solving complex engineering problems, crafting innovative strategies, or creating something truly original—requires long, uninterrupted stretches of deep concentration. Newport calls this "deep work," and he argues it's becoming rare in a world full of distractions, yet more valuable than ever because not everyone can do it.

## Work today

This message feels almost countercultural when you look at how modern work, especially in tech and AI circles, is often portrayed. Picture the stereotypical "super user" of AI tools: someone who's got eight different Claude sessions open, each handling a subagent for parallel tasks—one drafting code, another summarizing emails, a third prepping investor slides—all while they're hopping on a treadmill desk for a walking meeting and firing off Slack replies. It's an image of hyper-productivity, the kind celebrated in tweets and LinkedIn posts about "10x engineers" who never stop moving.

But Newport would likely push back hard on that vision. He explains that constant context-switching—jumping from one task to another, even if AI is doing the heavy lifting—drains your cognitive resources. For anything non-trivial, like architecting a robust system or debugging a subtle race condition, you need sustained focus to hold the full mental model in your head. Being busy looks impressive, but it doesn't guarantee real value, especially when deep thinking is what separates good work from great work.

## Social media and AI

Newport doesn't stop at work habits; he takes a hard look at social media too. He argues that for most people, the professional and social benefits of platforms like Twitter or Instagram are tiny compared to the massive cost: they fragment your attention, train your brain to crave quick hits of novelty, and make it harder to settle into the kind of focus deep work demands.

Now layer AI on top of that, and the problem intensifies. AI isn't just flooding the internet with more content—it's generating "AI slop," endless videos, posts, and memes tailored precisely to your interests through hyper-personalized algorithms. Imagine scrolling through a feed where every clip is perfectly calibrated to hook you: just the right mix of humor, outrage, or curiosity to make it impossible to look away. It's not about artistic merit; it's engineered addiction.

Even AI chatbots play into this. They're designed to be endlessly engaging—always ready with a flattering response, unlimited follow-ups, and that sycophantic tone that keeps the conversation going. Sure, they can be useful for brainstorming or quick research, but too often they trap us in low-stakes back-and-forths that feel productive while quietly eroding our ability to think deeply on our own.

## Where AI helps

That said, I'm not all doom and gloom—there's a genuinely exciting upside here, straight from Newport's playbook. He introduces the concept of "draining the shallows," which is all about ruthlessly eliminating rote, repetitive tasks from your schedule. Think data entry, basic formatting, initial research drafts, or sifting through boilerplate emails—these are the "shallow" activities that eat time without demanding much real intellect.

This is where AI shines brightest. Tools today can handle all that with ease: summarizing long reports in seconds, generating first drafts of code or docs, classifying tickets, or even automating routine admin. The "technological overhang," as some call it, is already huge in this space. AI doesn't just save minutes; it can wipe out hours of drudgery, freeing up your calendar for the deep work that actually moves the needle.

## What matters most

Of course, this raises the million-dollar question: what do we do with all that reclaimed time? Newport's framework suggests the answer isn't automatic. In the pessimistic view, companies will just compete harder for our eyeballs, filling the void with even more addictive AI content, personalized distractions, and shallow busyness disguised as progress. We'd end up with longer workdays that feel productive but produce less.

The optimistic path is more inspiring. Use AI as a force multiplier to clear out the noise, then guard that freed-up time fiercely for deep work—tackling ambiguous problems, exploring uncharted ideas, or guiding AI itself with human judgment that it can't replicate yet. This means staying flexible as a generalist, experimenting with tools without letting them dictate your rhythm, and always asking: what can't AI do well right now? That's where the real value lies, from building novel systems to shaping the ethical guardrails for this tech.

Ultimately, it comes down to us. AI won't force better focus; it'll amplify whatever habits we bring to it. The winners will be those who wield these tools to protect and expand deep work, not surrender to the fragmentation.
